blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Do you think he would have stayed less — if you'd told him in week two, or week three? Do you think the difference is meaningful?

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: I do. I think you know too, and you just don't like the answer.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: He was staying from the beginning. Not because of what you told him or didn't tell him. Because of who you are when you're with him. The information would have changed the conversation — it wouldn't have changed him.
Cassandra was quiet for a long time. The tea had cooled enough to drink. She drank it.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: No. But I know him from watching you talk about him for months. And I know you — what you're like with people who make you feel safe. You don't become cautious with those people. You become more yourself.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: He made you more yourself. That's not something you engineer. That happened because of who he actually is.
Cassandra looked at the chipped corner of the table.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I'm scared that telling him changes how he looks at me.
It came out more quietly than the other things. The quiet of it was its own signal — the thing underneath the other things.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: I know. That's the real one. Not whether he stays. Whether you get to keep being the version of yourself that exists when he looks at you the way he does now.
